entity: Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania
summary: I was going through the Wharton School of Accounting and Finance before I lost my job and decided to join the Service. It was part of my plan to get a college education, but circumstances pushed me into the military instead. Wharton represented my initial career path before the war changed everything.

I enlisted in the military at Philadelphia on September 26, 1940. It was the place where my military journey began, even though my family didn’t want me to join. Philadelphia was the starting point before I was sent to Fort Jackson for training.

After enlisting, I was sent to Fort Jackson, South Carolina, just outside Columbia. Back then, we lived in tents with buckets of water that would freeze in winter, which was tougher than I expected. Fort Jackson was where I went through my initial three months of training and got assigned to the Service company.

Fort Jackson was right outside Columbia, South Carolina, where I trained in 1940. Columbia was the nearby city to the base, and I remember the cold winters and the tent living conditions during my early service days. It was a formative place for my military experience.

My company commander at Fort Jackson was a West Point man, very military and strict, reminding me of General Patton. He was an excellent leader and took me under his wing, which helped me advance in the Service. His West Point background really showed in his leadership style.

My company commander reminded me a lot of General George S. Patton because of his military style and leadership. Patton’s reputation for being a strong leader was reflected in how my captain ran things, and the men respected him. That comparison stuck with me throughout my service.

Because of my abilities, my captain sent me to the First Army Headquarters to try for a position, though I didn’t get it. The experience was valuable, and it was part of how I advanced in the military. First Army was a step up in my service career around 1940.

We went on maneuvers in the Carolinas, including North Carolina, as part of our training before the war escalated. The maneuvers were intense and prepared us for what was to come. North Carolina was one of the key places where we practiced battlefield operations.

South Carolina was where Fort Jackson is located, and we also did maneuvers there. It was part of the Carolinas training circuit in 1940, where we lived in tents and learned the basics of military life. South Carolina was central to my early army experience.

Georgia was another state where we conducted military maneuvers before the war. Along with North and South Carolina, it was part of the southern training grounds that prepared us for combat. The experience in Georgia helped shape my readiness for deployment.

After older men were removed from our unit, we got new recruits from New York and New Jersey. These younger men came from places like New York, and it was a mix of people from the Northeast and other states. New York was part of the recruitment pool that refreshed our ranks.

New Jersey was another state where some of the younger recruits in my unit came from after the older men were removed. We had a mix of men from New Jersey, New York, and other states, which brought different backgrounds to the unit. New Jersey contributed to the fresh manpower in 1940.

There were some men from Tennessee in my unit, though most were from the Northeast. I remember one Southerner, probably from Kentucky, who would fall asleep standing in line, which was quite a sight. Tennessee was part of the diverse geographic makeup of our group.

Kentucky was represented in my unit by at least one Southerner who had some funny habits, like falling asleep leaning against a tree. It was interesting to see the mix of men from places like Kentucky alongside Northeastern recruits. Kentucky added to the diversity of our unit in 1940.

We received orders with the code word 'plum,' which we later learned meant the Philippine Islands. I was supposed to go there before Pearl Harbor was attacked. It was part of our deployment plan in late 1941, but the attack changed everything.

We crossed the continent by troop train and stopped in the Rocky Mountains for a while, where we played with snowballs. It was a memorable part of the journey to the West Coast before heading overseas. The Rockies were a brief but fun stop on our way to San Francisco.

We arrived in San Francisco where we prepared to board a Matson liner for deployment. The city was our last stop on the mainland before heading to the Pacific. San Francisco was where we loaded our equipment and got ready for what was ahead.

We boarded a Matson Navigation Company liner, which was a beautiful tourist ship with an American flag, not a typical troop ship. It was December 1941, just before Pearl Harbor, and we were preparing to sail to the Pacific. The Matson liner was our transport to Hawaii and beyond.

I was on the ship preparing to sail when Pearl Harbor was bombed on December 7, 1941. At first, I thought it was just another Orson Welles radio drama, but soon we heard the real news that the United States was under attack by the Japanese Navy. When we arrived at Pearl Harbor, the destruction was horrific, with the Arizona sunk and hangars destroyed. It was a tense and unforgettable experience arriving there just before Christmas.

When I first heard about the Pearl Harbor attack, I thought it was one of Orson Welles's radio shows, like 'The War of the Worlds,' which caused a lot of panic before. I laughed it off until I heard the real news on a portable radio. Orson Welles's broadcasts were well-known, so it was natural to think it was another dramatization at first.

entity: The War of the Worlds (radio drama)
summary: I remembered Orson Welles's 'The War of the Worlds' radio drama when I first heard about the Pearl Harbor attack and thought it might be something similar. That broadcast had famously caused panic by simulating an alien invasion, so it was on my mind when the news broke. It was only after hearing the bombs and announcements that I realized Pearl Harbor was really under attack.

The Japanese Navy was responsible for the attack on Pearl Harbor that we heard about while preparing to sail in December 1941. The announcer on the radio confirmed that the United States was under attack by the Japanese Navy, which changed our mission and plans entirely. Their assault caused massive destruction at Pearl Harbor and marked the U.S. entry into the war.

We arrived at the Hawaiian Islands shortly after Pearl Harbor was bombed. The islands were tense and dark at night with blackouts, and the military presence was strong. I traveled around Oahu and saw the devastation near Pearl Harbor and Hickam Field firsthand during Christmas 1941.

Our ship docked at Honolulu Harbor on the island of Oahu after Pearl Harbor was attacked. The harbor area had military towers and facilities, and it was where the troops disembarked while I stayed on the ship to manage equipment. Honolulu Harbor was the gateway to the Hawaiian Islands during that tense time.

We were stationed on the island of Oahu in the Hawaiian Islands after arriving post-Pearl Harbor. I went to Schofield Barracks on Oahu and saw the destruction around Hickam Air Force Base and Pearl Harbor. Oahu was where we trained and lived during that critical period in late 1941.

I was assigned to Schofield Barracks on Oahu after arriving in Hawaii. The barracks were open-air with screens instead of walls, and we dealt with bedbug infestations and blackouts. Schofield Barracks was our base for training and organizing after the attack on Pearl Harbor.

As we approached Hickam Air Force Base near Pearl Harbor, I saw the terrible destruction from the Japanese attack—hangars were ruined and planes destroyed. Hickam was a key military installation that suffered greatly on December 7, 1941. Witnessing that devastation was a sobering moment for me.

I saw the battleship Arizona sunk in Pearl Harbor after the attack. It was laying on its side among other damaged ships, a horrible sight that stayed with me. The Arizona was a symbol of the devastation inflicted that day.

M*A*S*H was not mentioned in my story or experience, so I have no direct connection or memories related to it during my service. My focus was on the events around Pearl Harbor and my military training and deployment.

I enlisted in the United States Army in 1940 at Philadelphia and went through training at Fort Jackson. I served in the Army throughout the early years of World War II, including deployment to Hawaii after Pearl Harbor. The Army was my career and the organization that shaped my wartime experience.

While I was in the Army, I had the opportunity to be an Army observer on a United States Navy destroyer during gunnery practice near Pearl Harbor. This gave me a unique perspective on Navy operations and training during the war. The Navy played a crucial role in the Pacific theater where I was stationed.
